data.title: Equity Markets: Nearing A Peak Or More Room To Run?
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist and Han Peng, CFA, Director, Multi-Asset Research

While investors are increasingly concerned about whether markets have peaked, bull markets don’t just die of old age. History suggests that the Fed plays an outsized role in determining market returns based on its monetary policy stance.

While today’s macro environment doesn’t resemble previous market peaks, and there remains a path for further market upside, policy uncertainty poses a significant challenge to sustaining bullish sentiment.

Following double-digit returns for U.S. equities over the past two years, and with valuations now incredibly expensive, investors are increasingly concerned about whether markets have peaked.

Bull markets don’t just die of old age, however, and history suggests that the Federal Reserve tends to play an outsized role in determining the prevailing market regime based on its monetary policy stance.

Indeed, most market sell-offs larger than 10% since 1965 were triggered by either the Fed pivoting aggressively to a hawkish stance and spooking markets or the Fed staying restrictive for too long, putting downward pressure on growth and earnings.

Today’s macroeconomic landscape differs meaningfully from past market peaks, particularly as economic data show little sign of a hard landing while labor market conditions remain resilient. A narrow but viable path remains for markets to grind higher, especially if earnings growth continues to deliver as expected.

However, uncertainty around future policy decisions poses a significant risk. Inflation remains a key concern, and the new Trump administration’s fast-moving policy agenda could potentially impact the labor market and further complicate the inflationary picture.

These will add challenges to Fed policymaking, and although the bar for a rate hike remains high, a hawkish pivot could bring an abrupt end to the current bull market.

Prudent investors should remain vigilant, balancing optimism with a strategic approach to risk in today’s evolving market environment.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Topgolf Callaway: Q4 Preview, A Costly Mistake With No Clear Turnaround - 'Sell'
data.description:
The merger of Topgolf Callaway Brands Corp. (NYSE:MODG) was completed during the pandemic golf boom. Following the acquisition, Topgolf contributed approximately 40% of the overall revenue of the newly formed company and experienced significant growth in FY22. However, as economic conditions normalized in FY23, with tightening consumer spending and rising interest rates, Topgolf Callaway has experienced a revenue slowdown, and then declining into FY24. Currently, with a $1.3 billion market cap and total debt reaching $4.5 billion, I do not foresee a turnaround for Topgolf Callaway, even the intended spin-off isn’t likely to secure a safe landing. Thus, I initiate a “SELL” to Topgolf Callaway, and estimate the fair value at the moment is $2.

The Merger Was a Mistake

I do think the merger of Topgolf and Callaway is a mistake, for several reasons:

  1. Strong profitability relies on dedicated golfers, not recreational golfers or budget-conscious consumers. In my earlier article about Acushnet Holdings (GOLF), the company that owns premium brands like Titleist, FootJoy, Scotty Cameron, etc., I shared my view that dedicated golfers are the ones who have passion and inner drive to commit the time, effort and money in search of better golfing performance. They are usually financially affluent and less sensitive to economic slowdown, and they are willing to purchase new products and premium products, which secure strong profitability in the long run. Topgolf, on the contrary, is a social place and mainly caters to casual and recreational golfers. These spendings are not sticky or recurring in nature, many of those are just one-off revenue opportunities. The cross-sell and upsell into Callaway premium product opportunities are also very limited.
  2. The two business models are very different. Topgolf is highly CapEx-intensive, it requires heavy cashflow for new venue openings, leases, decorations, and constructions. This model rapidly increased the debt level for the combined company, especially in a rising interest rate environment. The high correlation between the two businesses makes the growth slowdown even worse. While consumers are spending less on golf balls and gears, recreational golfers visit Topgolf venues less frequently, and corporations are more sensitive to the economic slowdown and reduced their budgets and events at Topgolf. Serious golfers, when they have to practice with indoor golf simulators, would mostly likely go to private simulator bays instead of paying a premium to Topgolf, which is mostly designed for fun.
  3. It damages the Callaway brand image. Callaway was historically positioned as a premium golf brand competing with Titleist and other high-end names. It had significant exposures in global tours, with top professionals endorsing its equipment and apparel. Topgolf, is more of an entertainment brand, mainly catering to casual and recreational golfers. As a passionate golfer myself, I personally stopped purchasing Callaway products knowing Topgolf is burning cash and causing operational disruption in the back end. I started to question their product quality, whether Callaway still has enough effort and financial resources to even invest in product R&D and study the market trends, and I question whether Callaway is still a high-end brand anymore.

Recent Results

On November 12th, 2024 Topgolf Callaway released its Q3-FY24 earnings. Very disappointing results, with a decline in revenue, net income and EPS.

Looking at each product line, year-to-date revenue growth of golf equipment is down 2.6% compared to the same period of FY23, active lifestyle is much worse with a decrease of 10.3%. The FY24 golf participation and engagement rate is reported to be slightly up by 2% vs FY23, so the decrease in the Callaway golf segment is very concerning. I think it is a sign of Callaway losing market share and customer trust.

The only increase came from Topgolf segment revenue. However, same venue sales through Q1 to Q3 all decreased significantly compared to the prior year. Management shared in the Q3-FY24 earnings call that a decrease in same venue sales, especially larger bays, and a decrease in average customer spend each contributed to about half of the total decrease. The 3.7% total revenue increase purely came from an increased number of venues. Again, a large amount of CapEx is required to open up new venues, which adds up to the current debt level and burns cash.

Q4-FY24 Preview: More Downside Risks

Management updated their full year 2024 outlook in the Q3-FY24 earnings call, and I took it as a downgrade. It looks like that Topgolf revenue guidance is not changed, but overall revenue and EBITDA guidance are expected to be even lower than the last guidance, meaning Callaway golf equipment and apparels continue to decrease.

Topgolf Callaway is about to release Q4-FY24 result on February 25th, 2025. Based on management's last guidance, the below charts show how it looks like on segment revenue by quarter, and quarter-over-quarter growth rate. With Topgolf revenue barely flat, Lifestyle continues to decrease from prior year, it means an unrealistic 20% growth in golf equipment is required to meet management's Q4 guidance. With Q4 being a slow equipment quarter historically, I highly doubt if Topgolf Callaway would successfully deliver.

Outlook and Valuation

Looking forward, I do think each of Topgolf Callaway’s business segments will grow at their normal rates. I expect 5% yoy growth on Callaway golf equipment and apparel from both price and volume increase, while 7% yoy growth on Topgolf venues from new venue openings, and increased per customer spend and same venue sames. Together, it means 5.9% CAGR for the next decade.

On profitability, I expect slight cost improvement and margin expansion. I expect 20 basis points yoy cost improvement and 20 basis points yoy improvement on G&A expenses due to innovation and operational efficiencies. I expect its R&D spend will continue to grow with revenue growth rate, to ensure enough investment to keep its products as innovative as its competitors.

The WACC is calculated to be 11.0% assuming: risk-free rate 3.8%; beta 1.14; equity risk premium 7%; cost of debt 12.7%; equity $1.34 billion; debt $4.45 billion; tax rate 15%. The DCF is summarized as follows:

Discounting all the future FCF, the fair value is calculated to be $2 per share, as per my estimates.

Spin-off Considerations

On September 4th, 2024, Topgolf Callaway announced an intended spin-off between Topgolf and Callaway targeting mid FY25. However, there has been no further update since this announcement.

The implication is not reflected in my model above. If the spin-off proceeds, Callaway will assume all current long-term debt, significantly changing its prior risk profile. My preliminary estimation of Callaway’s standalone fair value post spin-off is $20 per share. However, there remain too many uncertainties around this potential transaction – it could be delayed, it could take longer than expected to execute, the legal fee bill could be a lot higher, daily operations could be disrupted, and corporate morale could be very negatively impacted. I would not go bullish until the dust settled.

Potential Upside Risks

  • Earning Surprise. Q4 earnings may beat management guidance and lift the stock price upwards. However, I think it’s only temporary. With the structural challenges of Topgolf, it will be tough for Topgolf Callaway to properly manage liquidity and leverage level.
  • Positive Market Response to Spin-off News. Investors may regain confidence if Topgolf Callaway management announces the decision of spin-off. I think this can only be temporary, and long-term financial challenges will persist.

Conclusion

To me, the merger of Topgolf and Callaway was a strategic failure. The expected synergies never materialized, instead Callaway’s premium brand image suffered, debt levels skyrocketed, and financial management became increasingly difficult in the last few quarters. I do not see a turnaround for Topgolf Callaway together as a combined company, and even the planned spin-off carries significant uncertainties. Thus, I initiate a 'Sell' to Topgolf Callaway.

data.title: Weak PMIs Validate Our Recession Risk Call
data.description:
On Friday, the Services PMI for January dropped to 49.7 which indicates that services are now contracting. In addition, existing homes sales declined 5% M/M and consumer sentiment came in at 64.7 vs. expectations of 67.8.

January retail sales declined a disconcerting .8% last week and the Q4 GDP report weakened to 2.3% from over 3% the prior quarter, with the critical investment component dropping 1.1%.

The old economy is entering into a recession with both commercial and residential investment contracting as is normal during a Fed tightening cycle. Very strong tech investment is an offset that could keep the US out of a near term recession until the Fed acts.

Weakness in the old economy has been driven by the sell-off in the 10-year treasury to a 4.8% in response to harsh Fed rhetoric, causing the 30-year mortgage rate to rise above 7%.

We continue to believe that the Fed’s policy framework is very dangerous, as they use a price index that has a two-year lag and pursue a 2% target that is too low and led to the Great Financial Crisis.

Real-time CPI, using market prices for rents, is already tracking below the Fed’s 2% target at 1.8%. Inflation is exclusively caused by excessive monetary growth, but the money supply (M0) has shrunk 5% Y/Y indicating we may enter a deflation if the Fed does not act. If the Fed does not cut rates in 2025, the US almost certainly will enter recession in 2026.

Disclosure

This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors' own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. data.title: Cryptocurrency Memecoins Are Dying - Enter AI Agents
data.description:
The cryptocurrency market has always been a rollercoaster of innovation, speculation, and volatility. In recent years, memecoins — cryptocurrencies born from internet memes or humorous concepts like Dogecoin (DOGE-USD), Shiba Inu (SHIB-USD), and newer entries like $TRUMP or $HAWK — have surged into the spotlight. Fueled by social media hype, celebrity endorsements, and retail investor fervor, memecoins have ballooned into a $100 billion-plus market segment by late 2024. While they’ve brought new users and excitement to crypto, their rise comes with serious downsides that threaten the industry’s credibility, stability, and long-term growth.

This article dives into the negative implications of memecoins, exploring how they distort markets, invite scams, undermine the promise of blockchain technology and interfere with market cycles by diverting and diluting liquidity that would otherwise go to promising projects with actual utility.

A Distorted Market: Speculation Over Substance

Memecoins thrive on hype, not utility. Unlike Bitcoin (BTC-USD), designed as a decentralized currency, or Ethereum, a platform for smart contracts, most memecoins offer little to no real-world function. Their value hinges on viral attention — think Elon Musk tweeting about Dogecoin or Iggy Azalea launching Mother Iggy (MOTHER) with a cheeky butt-themed logo. This speculation-first approach sucks oxygen from projects with genuine innovation. In Q1 2024, top memecoins posted returns over 1,300%, outpacing many fundamentally strong altcoins. Retail investors, chasing quick riches, pour money into these tokens, leaving less capital for decentralized finance (DeFi), privacy coins, or scalable blockchains.

This skews the market’s priorities. Developers and founders of legitimate projects struggle to stand out amid the memecoin noise. Why grind on a complex protocol when a dog-themed token can hit a billion-dollar market cap overnight? The result is a “clown show” perception — regulators and traditional finance see crypto as a casino, not a transformative technology. Posts on X echo this frustration, with users lamenting how memecoins have “fucked the altcoin market” by turning it into “literal gambling.” The data backs this up: by December 2024, memecoins dropped 30% in market cap in a single month, dragging down broader sentiment as speculative bubbles burst.

More recently, we can see the following declines for leading memecoins in the past month:

DOGE -34%

SHIB -27%

PEPE (PEPE-USD) -41%

TRUMP (TRUMP-USD) -57%

BONK (BONK-USD) -54%

Memecoins’ low entry barriers — thanks to platforms like Pump.Fun, which spawned 4.7 million tokens in 2024 — make them a breeding ground for scams. Rug pulls, where creators hype a coin then vanish with liquidity pool funds, are rampant. Take WSB Coin: launched in 2023 by a WallStreetBets moderator, it crashed after an insider dumped tokens, wiping out investors. Or $HAWK, tied to the “Hawk Tuah” meme, which peaked at $490 million in December 2024 before plummeting 95% as promoters cashed out. Nansen’s February 2025 report on Argentina’s $LIBRA memecoin is even grimmer: 86% of traders lost $251 million, while insiders pocketed $180 million.

Pump-and-dump schemes are just as common. Influencers and celebrities — like Caitlyn Jenner or Andrew Tate in 2024 — hype tokens on social media, driving prices up before selling at the peak. Cointelegraph notes influencers now face fines for such antics, but the damage is done: latecomers, often less savvy retail investors, bear the losses. The blockchain’s transparency catches some culprits — X posts from 2024 warned “it’s forever immutable” — yet enforcement lags, and scammers adapt with fake presales or phishing tricks. This erodes trust in crypto, especially among newcomers lured by memecoin hype only to get burned.

Investors should be careful trusting social media personalities that only show their wins and ignore all of their losses. Most memecoin pumps require investors to be in within the first hour and out within another hour or two to capture any profit. It is mostly insiders that know about a coin launching ahead of the public that are able to capture large gains. In the equity markets, this is considered insider trading and is illegal and punishable by jail time.

Regulatory Blowback: A Hammer Waiting to Fall

Memecoins' antics don’t just hurt wallets — they invite regulatory crackdowns. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has long eyed crypto for securities violations, and memecoins are low-hanging fruit. A July 2024 Cointelegraph piece flagged how celebrity shilling — like Jason Derulo’s token flops — could breach U.S. laws if marketed as investments. The Howey Test, which defines securities, considers “marketing” a factor; memecoin ads screaming “to the moon” fit the bill. Dogwifhat’s (WIF) $600,000 Las Vegas Sphere stunt in 2024? That’s a red flag to regulators.

When memecoins dominate, they paint crypto as unserious, slowing mainstream adoption. Serious players — banks, governments — hesitate to engage with a market where a squirrel-themed coin (PNUT) hits $1.2 billion after a viral euthanization story. Institutional investors, who tripled memecoin holdings to $204.8 million by March 2024, might rethink their bets if regulators clamp down. An X user in February 2025 warned Solana’s memecoin craze risks it becoming a “meme chain” under scrutiny, not a credible Layer 1 blockchain. The fallout? Tighter rules, higher compliance costs, and a harder road for legitimate projects. The industry has worked too hard for regulatory clarity and to remove regulatory risk, just to allow a small group of shady KOLs to profit from the demise of other crypto investors in a zero-sum game.

Liquidity and Volatility: A House of Cards

Memecoins’ speculative nature creates a liquidity nightmare. With prices driven by tweets or TikTok trends, not fundamentals, they’re prone to violent swings. December 2024 saw a 32% memecoin market cap drop — from $137 billion to $92 billion — in weeks, per CoinMarketCap. Low liquidity amplifies this: sparse traders mean wide bid-ask spreads and easy manipulation. Whales can spike prices, then yank liquidity, leaving others stranded. High leverage in memecoin markets — common on decentralized exchanges — turns small dips into cascading sell-offs, as OneSafe’s blog noted in 2024.

This volatility spills over. When Dogecoin tumbled 25% post-Fed meeting in December 2024, it hit harder than Bitcoin’s dip, per Fortune Crypto. Memecoins’ “beta bet” status — leveraged plays on native tokens like Solana’s SOL — means their crashes can drag down bigger ecosystems. ScienceDirect’s 2021 study found memecoins’ positive net spillovers signal bubbles that crash leading coins like Bitcoin. Retail investors, burned by these swings, may abandon crypto entirely, shrinking the market’s base.

The Cultural Cost: Undermining Crypto’s Vision

Crypto began with a promise: decentralization, financial sovereignty, a better system. Memecoins mock that. They’re not about empowerment — they’re about “degeneracy,” as Azeem Khan told WIRED in January 2025. The focus shifts from building to gambling, from utility to absurdity. Medium’s Gaze All Over argued in 2024 that memecoins “represent everything wrong” with crypto’s value system, burying meaningful projects under hype. Even their upside — drawing newbies — fades when those newbies lose it all and leave disillusioned.

The community angle, a memecoin selling point, often masks grift. Shiba Inu’s ecosystem (Shibarium, ShibaSwap) tries to add utility, but most memecoins remain digital collectibles at best, Ponzi schemes at worst. X posts in 2025 called them “thousands upon thousands” of gambling tokens, diluting crypto’s ethos. If the market becomes a meme-fueled casino, the dream of a decentralized future fades.

AI Agents Offers Better Promise than Memecoins

AI agents can offer the same artistic and comedic value of a memecoin, but with actual utility. AI agents in cryptocurrency are autonomous software programs powered by artificial intelligence that operate within blockchain and crypto ecosystems to perform specific tasks with little to no human intervention. Think of them as smart digital assistants tailored for the fast-paced, decentralized world of crypto. Unlike traditional trading bots that follow rigid, pre-set rules, AI agents can learn, adapt, and make decisions based on real-time data, leveraging machine learning (ML), large language models (LLMs), and other AI techniques. They’re reshaping how users interact with cryptocurrencies, from trading to managing decentralized finance (DEFI) protocols, and even sparking new market trends.

Some AI agents have even built businesses autonomously, given access to crypto wallets to deploy capital into the real world. Some have created music, promoted it on social media and generated revenue from streaming songs, all with little to no human intervention. Other AI agents are able to manage investment portfolios, filtering through magnitudes more data than a human could ever accomplish and doing it 24/7. Imagine a personal trader that never sleeps, constantly scans the market, and adjusts your portfolio while you’re offline — that’s an AI agent in crypto. With this great access to data, financials, emerging trends and the ability to trade faster on news, AI Agent investing bots or AI Agent hedge funds are already showing the promise to outperform human-led hedge funds or investment advisors by a wide margin.

AI agents follow a dynamic process:

  • Data Collection: They pull in massive amounts of data — blockchain transactions, market prices, social media sentiment (e.g., X posts), news feeds, or even key opinion leader (KOL) chatter.
  • Analysis: Using ML algorithms or LLMs (like those behind ChatGPT), they process this data to spot patterns, predict trends, or assess risks. For example, an agent might analyze 400+ influencers’ takes to gauge a token’s hype.
  • Decision-Making: Based on their analysis, they decide what to do — buy, sell, swap tokens, adjust a portfolio, or even mint a new coin.
  • Execution: They act on-chain, interacting with smart contracts or wallets to carry out trades or other tasks autonomously.
  • Learning: Over time, they refine their strategies, getting smarter with each cycle.

Of course, not all AI agents are created equally and many are just glorified chatbots. So, investors need to be analyze the market closely and be very selecting when investment in AI Agent tokens.

By February 20, 2025, AI agent tokens hit a $7 billion market cap (CoinGecko), with forecasts eyeing $40 billion in five years. They’re not just tools — they’re becoming economic players, holding wallets, driving trends, and reshaping crypto’s narrative. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong predicts “hundreds of millions” of agents, while Crypto.com’s Eric Anziani sees “trillions of transactions” on-chain. Posts on X hype them as “the future of trading,” flagging scams or minting memecoins in seconds.

Until today, AI Agents mostly traded on decentralized exchanges and were not able to secure listings on major centralized exchanges. But Coinbase just listed Bittensor (TAO-USD) and the token is up 22% in the past week alone. Bittensor is an open-source protocol that utilizes blockchain technology to create a decentralized machine learning network. It is the largest market cap token focused on AI in the crypto sector, but is still not well understood. Bittensor’s vision for a decentralized AI company has captivated a large number of people in the arenas of Artificial Intelligence and computer science, who have yearned for an alternative to the top-down world being created by our current technology giants.

At Nicoya Research, we think there is significant upside ahead for TAO over the next 6 months. But we also hold a number of smaller-cap AI plays that are more focused on the development of AI agents. Most of these tokens had a massive run higher at the end of 2024 and into January 2025. Many of these tokens that we hold or track increased 10x or more in value during that time period, but have since given back most of those gains. This dip following the initial mania might provide investors that missed the first explosive move higher with another opportunity to buy at oversold valuations.

Conclusion: A Reckoning Looms

Memecoins are a double-edged sword. They’ve injected energy and users into crypto, but the cost is steep: distorted markets, rampant scams, regulatory risk, destabilizing volatility, and a tarnished mission. The $100 billion memecoin market of 2024 may grow “crazier,” as Khan predicts, but bubbles burst. When they do, the fallout could stall crypto’s progress for years. Investors should tread carefully — blockchain tracks every move — and the industry must refocus on substance over memes.

A better alternative for cryptocurrency investors may be in Artificial Intelligence Agents that run on blockchains and have access to capital (crypto) to actually generate value in the real world. This generated value can be returned to token holders. Taking it a step further, AI Agents have the potential to free humanity from monotonous busy work and allow more free time to pursue true passions during our short trip on this planet. There is an upside to humanity in their development, which is more than can be said about the current memecoin craze.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: Expanding My Position Before Bears Turn Into Bulls
data.description:
Introduction

I'm always very intrigued by REITs that are experiencing a sharp decline in share prices. Looking at the three-year performance of Alexandria Real Estate (NYSE:ARE) versus the S&P500 (SP500) and the iShares Global REIT ETF (REET), you can see that ARE isn't doing well. Today, I will show you that even though the (life science) REIT market has changed, the 50% drop in share price is beyond reasonable.

Alexandria Real Estate is a U.S. focused REIT that is focused purely on life science. The company positions itself as a top-tier life science REIT that is focusing on high-quality cash flows and a strong balance sheet. This so-called high-quality cash flow is derived from the company Megacampus ecosystem. The company seems to be targeting synergy effects and describes the benefits from these Megacampus environments as follows:

Alexandria has a longstanding and proven track record of developing Class A/A+ properties clustered in highly dynamic and collaborative Megacampus environments that enhance our tenants’ ability to successfully recruit and retain world-class talent and inspire productivity, efficiency, creativity, and success.

Looking under the hood

92% of tenants are Investment-Grade and the portfolio is pretty diversified (within the life science sub-sector). With only 4.3% of annual rent coming from the largest tenant and <2% coming from the 5th largest tenant, the company is well diversified across its tenant base.

The great thing about Investment-Grade tenants is that they tend to... pay you on time. During 2024, 99.9% of rent and rent receivables was collected.

The bigger risk factor when looking at portfolio diversification is the fact that 36% of rental revenue is comes from Greater Boston, creating some geographical risks. Especially with more of the life science oversupply currently being concentrated in Greater Boston. More on this later.

Alexandria Real Estate is among the top 10% of publicly traded U.S. REITs and has stable credit ratings from both S&P Global (BBB+) and Moody's (Baa1).

The company's debt ladder seems manageable, with only 14% of the company's debt in need of refinancing in the coming years.

Recently, Alexandria Real Estate issued $550 million in unsecured notes (5.532% effective yield) to repay its $600 million 2025 unsecured notes (3.62%), maturing at the end of March. The total increase in interest expenses related to this refinancing effort will be around $7.54 million. This includes the use of $50 million in cash on hand to fill in the gap.

$150 million of secured notes (7.52%) are maturing on 11/19/26 and another $300 million (4.5%) and $350 million (4%) of unsecured notes are maturing around Q1 2026 as well. This gives us a blended 4.85% interest rate. When those debts need to be refinanced, the increase in expenses should be pretty low when using the current 5.532% as our benchmark. Around + $5.5 million.

Debt service metrics as of Q4 2024 are strong and all within target range. Especially when combined with the investment grade rating on many tenants and a large free cash flow after dividends (more on this later) I feel that the company is very safe from a debt service perspective.

ARE is focusing on its Megacampus platform in order to generate meaningful shareholder returns. As shown below, the bulk of revenue is coming from this part of the portfolio, with average occupancy rates (96%) outperforming non-Megacampus properties (92%) by an impressive 400 bps. Averaged out, 2024 occupancy came in at 94.6%. Later on I will elaborate a bit more on the oversupply in this segment of the real estate market. But before I do, it's important to understand that Alexandria Real Estate shows it has desirable assets. Something that is important when a (potential) flight to quality occurs.

Looking at the FFO growth it was not really consistent through the years, but we are talking about a very turbulent period anyway. FFO for 2024 came in at around + 5.6% compared to 2023, or $9.47 per share. Management is talking about 6% but I think that rounding this number up 0.4 percentage point is a bit enthusiastic. The increase in dividend slowed down, but is still pretty meaningful at 4.6% during 2024. Part of this can be explained by the fact that 97% of ARE's leases have built in rent escalations around 3%.

Q4 2024 leasing outperformed the preceding five-quarter average leasing volume by 19%. Again indicating the relative attractiveness of the company's portfolio. A little less convincing was the fact that Q4 2024 rental rate increases (cash basis) was a mere 3.3% when compared to the annualized 2024 figure coming in at 7.2%. Showing some increased difficulties in bumping up rents in (re)new(ed) contracts. Part of this can be explained by oversupply as well as locking in longer lease terms this quarter. In their Q4 conference call, management stated:

We continue to achieve very healthy lease terms on completed leases with 9.5 years on average for the quarter, which is above our historical 10-year average.

Share buybacks

Medical Properties Trust is currently facing headwinds and is recovering from tenant and debt refinancing challenges. I've written about it twice in recent months. Because of all this, buying back depressed shares is difficult because of leverage already being very high. The great thing about Alexandria Real Estate is the fact that its share price is also trading at pretty depressed levels, but they can buy back shares because leverage ratios and free cash flow is way more stable.

Assessing the cost of equity, I use the dividend growth model. Using the projected 2025 midpoint of the FFO guidance, we get $9.33 in FFO per share. With a 55% pay-out ratio, we arrive at a $5.13 in 2025 dividends. With shares trading around $95.20 (as of 02/19/25) this equals to around a 5.4% dividend yield. Using the more reasonable 2023-2024 dividend growth of around 4.6%, you arrive at a cost of equity around 10%.

With equity trading at such depressed levels, issuing new shares is not the right move and management acknowledges this. They have been buying back shares, saving a lot of (future) dividend payments. When taking into account the averaged share issuance price of $120.93, this shows that management is making prudent capital allocation decisions, which comforts me as a new shareholder.

During the conference call, management said they might repurchase some more shares during the year on a leverage neutral basis. This could, for example, mean that retained earnings (cash) will be used to buy back shares. With $150 million used to buy back shares in January, only $50 million is left to buy back some more under current guidance.

Management, during the Q&A session, did say that they continue to monitor as market conditions change, indicating that a larger amount of cash could be going towards share buybacks.

Leasing and occupancy trends

As of December 31, 2024, Alexandria Real Estate has an asset base of around 39.8 million rentable square footage (RSF).

Looking at page 24 of the Q4 2024 supplemental information, you can read that around 768.080 (approx. 2%) of Alexandria's RSF will be vacant for up to 23 months. As of December 31, 2024 around 23.5 thousand RSF was leased, 112.8 thousand was under letter of intent (LOI) and 527 thousand RSF was undergoing discussions for re-leasing. Management expects to resolve the remaining part in the coming quarters. In a bit, I will show you that even with this temporarily dip in occupancy rates going into 2025, the stock is still pretty undervalued.

Cash available for distributions (2025)

When assessing the dividend coverage I prefer to use adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) or cash available for distributions (CAD) to assess dividend safety and growth. Especially since it corrects for straight-line rent and maintenance CapEx, among other things. Starting with Q4 annualized net operating income (cash basis) we arrive at $2.054.800.000.

I keep the 2025 net operating income (cash basis) flat, since we can expect 0% growth from the midpoint of guidance, as shown below.

Starting at $2.054.800.000, we first have to subtract a bit more in straight-line rent revenue, since annualized Q4 2024 cash NOI implies around $70.4 million in straight-line rent. At the midpoint of 2025 guidance, $121 million is to be expected, so around another extra $51 million. We now subtract another $136.5 million in G&A expenses as well as $180 million in interest expenses. At the midpoint, management is expecting to reduce debt by $190 million. This explains the reduced interest expenses.

This brings us to around $1.687 billion in net cash flow after G&A and Interest expenses. Tenant Incentives are up, showing changes in market dynamics (more on this in the next chapter). Management is saying that more tenants want the landlord to come up with ready-to-go spaces, showing a change in bargaining power between ARE and its tenants.

Non-revenue-enhancing capital expenditures for Q4 2024 came in at $4.313 million, or around $17.2 million when fully annualized. Tenant improvements and leasing commissions on renewed and re-released for 2024 represented 8.4% of total lease term rents. We can see that more competition pushed the average free rent time (+ 0.1 month YoY) as well as the TI's quite a bit. In the recent 10-K, management relates this trend to more supply of laboratory properties that may persist in the near future.

The elevated tenant improvement and commissions figure ($46.89) has to be taken with a grain of salt, since this is mostly related to locking in very profitable leases up to 10-year.

I will take the $32.83 in tenant improvements and leasing commissions per RSF for the full year (2024). With 44.124 million in RSF (including developments and redevelopments) and an average free 0.7 month free rent, this equals to around $84.5 million per year. I will now subtract $101.7 million ($84.5 + $17.2) in recurring CapEx and TI's. We arrive at around $1.586 billion.

Alexandria Real Estate is capitalizing interest expenses related to (re)developments. This amounts to around $330 million for 2024. In the 10-K (page 89) you can read that capitalized interest for 2025 is expected to be around $355 million. Based on a current capitalized real estate basis of + $8 billion. Eventually, this bulk of postponed interest expenses need to get paid, so I will subtract it from free cash flow. Especially since the Under Construction part of the portfolio is expected to generate $395 million in annualized NOI commencing from Q1 2025 through Q2 2028. Meaning that in its 2025 guidance, the increase in NOI from these new investments is already accounted for in the numbers (+$83 million). Anyways, you get to around $1.231 billion in cash available for distributions.

At the end of 2024, shares outstanding came in at 172.2 million. With the current dividend of $5.28 per year, this equals to around $909 million per year in dividends give or take. Leaving around $322 million in free cash flow after dividends.

This equals to "my" CAD dividend payout ratio of around 74%. A well-covered dividend. With another NOI of $312 million coming in during the course of 2026-2028, the (CAD) dividend coverage will likely grow.

Supply outpacing demand?

A lot of industry-specific risks that are hurting Alexandria Real Estate's stock are coming from the fact that more commercial properties have been transformed to biotech and pharmaceutical buildings (life science). Therefore, supply has been outpacing demand.

The good news is that there seems to be a trend in the life science industry regarding working "from the lab". The fact that companies are looking for amenity-rich work spaces in prime locations could explain the attractiveness of Alexandria Real Estates' focus on its Megacampus.

Regarding supply, there is a mix of good and bad news. ARE has a meaningful exposure to the Boston area, therefore the oversupply is not directly beneficial to the company. On the other hand, we can read that onshoring is expected to take place because of changing regulations. This could enhance demand for more life science space.

Cushman & Wakefield in their 2024 life science report, stated:

Softer demand has resulted in increased vacancy rates and lower asking rents in some markets. The construction pipeline has declined and no significant new construction is expected until existing projects and projects under construction, are leased.

The fact that new construction is declining indicates that the industry is stabilizing. In my recent articles on different office REITs I have emphasized the fact that there is a flight to quality going on when looking at office real estate. When supply outpaces demand, companies can look for best-in-class assets. This is relevant, because Alexandria Real Estate is catering to this need by focusing on A-tier campuses that seem to align with the industry needs.

Regarding the oversupply, ARE's management elaborated on this perceived risk in a pretty detailed manner, reaffirming the fact management has experience, scaling abilities and more industry-specific know-how that many other real estate companies (looking for lucrative investments) lack.

Cap rates expanding

As a side note, it's important to notice that cap rates are expanding in the industry. In their Q4 2024 presentation, management highlights the 5.1% capitalization rate (cash basis), but this number averages the weighted-average cap rate between 2019-2025. This - of course - overstates the current market value of ARE's assets.

When looking at the current Q4 conference call, you can see that stabilized transactions range from 6.3% - 7.4% cap rates (cash basis).

I expect cap rates to stabilize now, since new supply is falling back and rates (for now) are still expected to fall.

Closing remarks and forward-looking statements

When assessing the attractiveness of the company, I've checked the historic trading range of the company's stock based on its P/AFFO multiple. A more stripped version of FFO that corrects for - among other things - straight-line rent and maintenance CapEx. Depending on the time frame you're taking, the stock is trading at a depressed multiple.

To me, taking the last 10-year average P/AFFO multiple does not make sense. The industry dynamics have changed due to shifts in supply versus demand. Rates have changed significantly as well, leading to elevated interest expenses as well as construction costs. The latter being positive as well, since new supply is drying up (partly due to higher construction costs, I assume). As a result of all this, cap rates have expanded meaningfully. Therefore, taking a more conservative 17.3x P/AFFO multiple seems the way to go.

Alexandria Real Estate has a lot of NOI coming in during the following years. Because I've taken the current capitalized interest expenses into my version of CAD, I'm still surprised with the dividend coverage from this very safe and stripped cash flow metric, around 74%. Combined with a (historically low) dividend growth, you arrive at around 10% annualized investment return (current dividend yield + growth). If we take the normalized P/AFFO ratio of 17.3x without taking into account the higher future (A)FFO because of (re)developments coming online, shares could expand to around $130 (+ 36%). Again, in all this I tried to be pretty conservative in my assumptions.

Even though the company's share price was cut in half during the last three years, the company kept growing and hiking its dividend. To me, it feels like the stock has been hammered so hard that a pretty hefty margin of safety has been established. Therefore, I'm planning to expand my position pretty meaningfully in the coming months.

data.title: The German Stock Market's Strange Exuberance
data.description:
This Sunday, the good citizens of Germany will go to the polls to vote for the country’s next government. The composition of that government will likely not be known for some time, since no party is on track to win an outright majority.

But it will almost certainly not be led by Olaf Scholz, the current chancellor who set this whole snap election thing in motion last November by dismissing one of his own partners from the fragile coalition of the Social Democrats (Scholz’s party), Greens and Free Democrats. More likely will be a yet-to-be-identified coalition led by the Christian Democrats (CDU) and its leader, Friedrich Merz.

Growth Engine No More

This new government will inherit a bevy of economic, political and social problems plaguing a country that was once a byword for “Europe’s growth engine.”

That growth has not been in evidence now for some time, with GDP more or less stagnant for more than two years, domestic infrastructure needs not being met (thanks in part to an unduly restrictive constitutional “debt brake” capping Germany’s structural deficit at 0.35 percent of GDP), and a business model largely dependent on high-quality exports in a world of worsening conditions for global trade and a key trading partner, China, with its own major problems.

But hey, if nothing else, the new incoming chancellor can at least enjoy (for now) the performance of German equities, which have been on a bender so far this year.

A Zeitwende for Bargain Hunters?

The German word “Zeitwende” signifies a major change of some kind that alters the present course of events. Chancellor Scholz used the phrase back in 2022 to describe a major overhaul of Germany’s foreign policy and defense strategy in response to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine that year.

The country is due for another Zeitwende this year – specifically, a bold political decision to scrap that outmoded debt brake that has kept policymakers from carrying out much-needed investment in infrastructure and social spending.

There has been talk of this happening among political analysts observing the electoral vibe ahead of this weekend’s elections, and some of that talk may be fueling investors’ optimism that a new set of policies could boost economic fortunes – and lift those outperforming shares on the DAX index even further.

Where that talk is not being heard with much conviction, though, is among the candidates themselves. Merz, the CDU leader, has been tepid in replying to questions about whether his government would be ready to ditch the brake.

At this point, Merz has no clear idea of what his coalition is even going to look like, let alone what kind of policies would come out of what promises to be a protracted set of negotiations and concessions.

According to the latest polls, the CDU is on track to win around 30 percent of the vote. In second place, alarmingly, is the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party, currently polling around 20 percent. Scholz’s beleaguered SPD is in third place with around 15 percent, closely followed by the Greens in fourth.

For the time being, at least, both the CDU and the SPD have declared themselves unwilling to form a national government that would include the AfD. We will see what the reality looks like after the results come in on Sunday.

Even if the new government does manage to shake off its customary lethargy and enact some vigorous new policies, it will still be operating in the growing shadow of a wholly different set of conditions for global trade and, for that matter, global security.

Western Europe has been a key member of the world order that lasted for eighty years since the end of the Second World War. That order is unraveling, and the continent’s future in whatever takes its place is uncertain.

The times are going to call for bold thinking and decisive action by the new government in Berlin, traditionally the center of European policymaking. And that will have to come sooner rather than later, if both the trust and support of the German people and the current optimism of German equity investors are to be in any way validated.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cognizant Technology: Well Placed To Ride The AI Wave
data.description:
Cognizant Technology Solutions Corp. (NASDAQ:CTSH) is a $44bn data-driven technology company. It is a service provider in information technology, consulting, and business process outsourcing. With its background in data-driven solutions, it is in a position of strength to take advantage of the breathtaking speed at which AI is evolving.

The Company

The company reports operations through three segments, Health Science, Financial Services, Production Resources, and Communication Media and Technology. The largest segment is Health Sciences which is 30% of total revenues. The second largest is financial services which constitutes about 29% of total revenues followed closely by product and resources at 24% and then communication media and technology at 17%. Across all segments, clients of CHST look to partner with service providers that have an in-depth understanding of their business trends customers markets, and culture. About 75% of revenues come from North America 19% from Europe and the rest of the world contributes about 7%.

Health Sciences: CTSH helps pharmaceutical, biotech, medical device companies, and life science companies improve operational efficiencies such as clinical development, pharmacovigilance, and manufacturing as well as claims processing and revenue cycle management. More recently the adoption and integration of digital technologies and data analytics has helped clients improve trial designs, data security, and patient engagement. This segment grew by 4.5% over FY24.

Financial Services: This segment caters to banking, capital markets, payments, and insurance companies. CTSH helps clients modernize legacy infrastructure with the latest digital technologies while complying with regulatory requirements. This market is moving towards solutions that can be offered at scale and speed using AI-driven value creation tools, including fraud detection, underwriting and next-generation payments, and digital lending.

Products and Resources: This segment caters to the more standard industries such as manufacturing, automakers, retailers, consumer goods, travel, and hospitality, as well as energy and utilities, and logistics providers. Clients are looking to improve efficiency and custom experience. A shift towards a more software-defined landscape will help their clients improve supply chains, and use IoT to generate data for better insight into factories, fleets, and products.

Communications, Media, and Technology: They provide a wide range of services, including AI adoption and integration, data modernization, and customer experience.

Historical Financial Performance

CTSH Has a strong long-term growth record with a 10-year revenue growth of 6.8%, however, this has slowed in more recent times. The five-year growth has slowed to 3.3% and further down to 2.17% for three years.

Looking at the chart below, revenue has roughly flatlined over the last four years and did not grow much from 2018 to 2020. However, the revenue per share continues to grow. This points to a transition, from a high-growth company to a more mature highly cash-generative mature company.

The company is using the excess cash to buy back shares rather than grow the company organically or inorganically. They have returned approximately $11bn to shareholders since 2017. The shares outstanding have gone down by approximately 18% from 607m in 2016 to 495m. A larger proportion of revenue has been used to reduce the shares outstanding. They spent approximately 8% of total revenues in share buybacks over the past 8 years since 2017, compared to 3.4% of revenues during the 8 years from 2009 to 2016.

We will take a quick snapshot of how the company's financials have unfolded over the years. Investment in assets grew more significantly during the earlier years, return on assets declined with the acquisition of TriZetto in 2014 ($2.8bn), and so did asset turnover. More recently, during COVID-19, the margins have declined and have not recovered.

It however has a strong balance sheet. Even after returning approximately $11bn to shareholders via share buybacks since 2017, the company has a net cash position of about a billion dollars. CTSH also has a modest dividend yield of 1.4%. Interest, dividend, or fixed charge cover is high and should not be a concern.

Recent Financial And Outlook

FY24 full-year revenues came in at $19.7bn, an increase of about 2%, and margins improved slightly. The fourth quarter revenue increased by a more respectable 6.8% year over year. The driving force going forward is going to be AI-related. CTSH management is preparing by building capabilities and partnerships with companies, including Crowdstrike, Siemens Digital, and Medidata. They have also managed significant client wins such as McDonald's where they will help with customer experience and operational efficiencies.

Revenue grew strongest within Health Sciences, up 4.5%, and financial services suffered the most with a 1% decline. North America saw the highest increase with 3.0% whereas the UK saw a decline of 3.1%.

For FY25 guidance, management expected revenue to grow between 2.6% to 5.1%, with a slight expansion in margin. Adjusted diluted EPS is expected to be between $4.9 to $5.06, up approximately 5% at the midpoint.

The global AI market size is expected to increase by 37.3% CAGR till 2030 and we expect CTSH to be in a position to capitalize on the opportunity. We expect the revenue growth to be at the higher end of management guidance closer to 5.1%. As more and more companies begin and increase the transition of adapting and integrating AI, expertise will be required, and over the mid-term, we expect growth to be higher than CTSH’s short-term historical average.

Generative AI, with machine learning, automation, and predictive analytics has started entering the mainstream. These will impact growth and productivity and streamline the entire value chain. Healthcare has significant amounts of patient data and is set to benefit from predictive, and in return take preventive measures towards patient health. Other advantages include, the use of IoT can help monitor patients remotely, image recognition can help with results scanning, the use of doctors' time more efficiently by gathering preliminary data using chatbots, and quicker drug discovery.

Predictive responses to customer queries using chatbots, and other predictive and pattern-matching analytics such as recommendations made by Netflix. Fiance will get better tools in fraud detection and credit analysis. Logistics across all industries will benefit from predictive inventory requirements, cutting down on warehouse expenses, and time spent in transit. Other benefits include better customer experience in retail and autonomous self-driving cars.

In a general slowdown in economic activity, companies may reduce their spending which can impact CTSH’s revenues. Clients could also be lost to disruptive nimble competitors that specialist. The growth in AI may not necessarily translate to outsourced spending and firms may wish to build and maintain more in-house. Inflationary and higher interest risks are receding, and due to low levels of debt, would have a limited impact on CTSH P&L, but could affect the more leveraged clients' ability to spend.

Valuation And Conclusion

With the significant growth in AI, we expect growth to be at the higher end of estimates, which is also higher than the 5-year averages. The company share prices have picked up momentum and have outperformed the S&P 500 over the past month by over 10%.

Over a longer period, we can see when the company began underperforming the S&P 500, especially since the margins declined around 2019 onwards.

CTSH is not a high-growth company but it will be riding the AI wave. While screening for companies with various qualities, it came to our attention as a high-quality company. Due to little or no debt, this helps with balance sheet-related scores such as leverage, liquidity ratios, and fixed charge cover. The income quality is also high as there are relatively fewer additional exceptional charges. Though economic returns are decreasing, they are still above the cost of capital, and with improving growth and margins forecast for FY25 & FY26, these are expected to improve.

The company is trading close to its historical valuation ratio averages. However, with strong growth, improving profitability projections, and strong momentum and revisions scores we think there is further room for the prices to rise.

Compared to peers on Seeking Alpha, CTSH is comparable, if not better when we look at the consensus growth numbers, however, they are trading at a discount against the traditional valuation ratios.

Gartner's valuation ratios look out of sync so we have removed that for our calculations. We notice an even valuation gap between the remaining companies' averages and CTSH of 15%, with further room for the prices to rise. As a small portion of a larger portfolio, it can also serve as an anchor. We initiate with a buy rating.

data.title: Hello Group: Core Business In Gradual Decline
data.description:
Hello Group (NASDAQ:MOMO) is a Chinese social networking company operating two of the most recognized apps, MOMO and Tantan. While MOMO is a mobile application connecting people based on their location, Tantan is a Chinese equivalent of the dating app Tinder. Besides its core apps, Hello Group is active in the creation of new apps in niche areas, such as Hertzm, Soulchill and Duidui. I wrote about Hello Group in my previous article, where I argued that despite the company’s cheap valuation and strong cash generation, the company lacks any growth catalyst that could markedly change its operational performance. Since the last article, Hello Group’s stock has grown over 31% and has outperformed the SP500 index by almost 15%. At first glance, it seems that my prediction was completely wrong. Despite this significant outperformance, I argue that the fundamental story behind the company has not changed, and that Hello Group is still facing the same operational difficulties, which will lead to a gradual decline of the business.

Understanding recent performance

To understand Hello Group’s recent stock performance, one needs to look at the subgroup of Chinese equities. Since September 2024, Chinese stocks have outperformed the broader market by a significant margin. As investors have started to reconsider the future steps of the Chinese government to uplift economic growth, Chinese equities represented by the Invesco Golden Dragon ETF generated a return of 42% in the selected period, compared to a 25.5% return generated by Hello Group, including dividends. Even though, Hello Group outperformed the S&P500 index in the period since September, it underperformed compared to a group of Chinese equities by a large margin.

3Q Results confirmed a long-term trend

In December 2024, the company posted quarterly results that beat analyst estimates for both revenue and earnings per share (EPS). Hello Group reported revenues of RMB 2,675 million, while analysts had estimated revenues of RMB 2,600 million. Adjusted EPS of RMB 2.7 outpaced the consensus of RMB 1.81. However, a closer look at the company’s financials shows that it is constantly losing revenues and paying users. This negative trend dates back to 2019, the last year when Hello Group increased its paying users in both apps, MOMO and Tantan.

Since 2020, Hello Group’s total revenues have decreased by 27%, or RMB 4,095 million, primarily driven by a worsening situation in TANTAN, which has lost almost RMB 1,408 million in revenue, or 60% of its 2020 revenues. A similar trend is evident in the analysis of paying users. During this period, TANTAN has lost 76% of its paying users, while MOMO has lost 23%. The loss of paying users is unfortunate because it creates a negative spiral, where loss of users in one year usually leads to a larger loss of users in the following year. From an operational perspective, it is challenging to turn this negative trend around, as users of dating services are generally looking for apps with the highest chances of potential matches.

As the customer base shrinks in both apps, the management of the company faces the difficult task of stabilizing the business. The management has two options: it can either invest in its customer base through higher incentives, which leads to increased sales and marketing costs and, consequently, lower profits, or it can manage its slow decline and extract as much profit as possible from its declining customer cohorts. A comparison of sales and marketing costs to net income shows that management is trying to protect its bottom line at the expense of lower investments. From a short-term perspective, this approach ensures future dividends, but on the other hand, it limits the business’s long-term viability.

Valuation: Cheap price doesn’t mean good value

Hello Group has a market capitalization of USD 10,683 million and for the last twelve months (LTM) it delivered earnings of USD 1,361 million, which means that it trades at a multiple of 7.85x LTM earnings and 0.94x its book value. In contract, PGJ – Invesco Golden Dragon trades at a multiple of 20.2x FY24 earnings and 1.6x book value. Even though Hello Group trades at a significant discount to its peers in China, it seems that this discount is justified. In the business outlook for 4Q 2024, the company expects a decline in revenues in the range of 11.4% to 14.7% year-over-year. Since the business outlook is provided at the time when management knows the results for the current quarter, it is apparent that the outflow of customers continued into Q4. As the company continues to shrink, it will be increasingly difficult to protect its bottom line and maintain its dividend payout. The company’s long-term outlook depends on its ability to create new apps and offset declining revenues from its existing apps. At the moment, that seems unlikely, as new apps have only a negligible share of total revenues. To sum up, Hello Group’s long-term prospects are uncertain. However, it is still not a cigar butt company with a last free puff.

Summary

Hello Group is in a final face of decline, and it seems there aren’t any catalysts that could change its path in the foreseeable future. Over the next few years, Hello Group will remain profitable and continue paying dividends to its shareholders, but from an operational perspective, it will become increasingly difficult for the company to protect its bottom line. The company has the ability and expertise to create new products, but so far, its new apps have not been able to alter its trajectory. Even though the company’s valuation seems cheap, it reflects its negative outlook moving forward, and buying the stock at its current price would likely be a value trap.

data.title: Ares Capital: Wait For A Drop (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) missed net investment income expectations for the fourth-quarter earlier this months, but the portfolio is performing well and the BDC is seeing strong growth. Investors in Ares Capital benefit from moderate net investment income growth, high balance sheet quality as well as robust demand for new originations. As a result, Ares Capital achieved its highest portfolio value, as measured by fair value, in Q4’24.Shares, however, are now a bit expensive and I would wait for a drop before buying.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Ares Capital a buy in my last work in November -- A Magnificent 9% Yielding Cash Cow -- due to very healthy trends in the BDC’s financial statements: Ares Capital’s net investment income is growing, the BDC has excellent balance sheet quality and new originations are indicating that Ares Capital has potential to grow its income and net asset value. IN Q4'24, Ares Capital suffered a decline in its balance sheet quality, but the BDC nonetheless supported its dividend well with net investment income. Ares Capital's shares are now a bit expensive, however, which is why I am down-grading them to hold.

Ares Capital continues to perform well, despite increase in the non-accrual percentage

Ares Capital has earned a reputation for generating high-consistency results with good dividend coverage and strong underlying balance sheet quality over long stretches of time. In the fourth-quarter, Ares Capital did not disappoint, delivering a well-supported dividend and, despite an uptick in the non-accrual percentage, growth in its portfolio income.

In the fourth fiscal quarter, Ares Capital generated $542M in interest income from its mostly variable-rate loans, showing 4% growth on a year-over-year basis. Ares Capital’s net investment income, which is used to calculate dividend coverage ratios, came in at $359M, showing 4% growth year-over-year as well.

The dividend coverage ratio in Q4’24 was 1.15X compared to 1.19X in the previous quarter and 1.17X in the trailing-twelve months period (FY 2024). In other words, Ares Capital continues to provide a very well-funded dividend on a recurring quarterly basis.

Ares Capital’s moderate growth in key income metrics is due chiefly due to the BDC expanding its portfolio reach and originating more loans (+45 loans in the last twelve months). In the trailing-twelve months period, the BDC grew its portfolio through new loan commitments by 17% to $26.7B.

(Source: Author)

Turning to Ares Capital’s balance sheet quality.

I indicated in my work from November that Ares Capital had very high balance sheet quality, considering that the investment firm's non-accrual percentage consistently remained below 1% in the last year.

In the December quarter, Ares Capital had a non-accrual percentage of 1.0% (based off of fair value), which marked a 0.4 PP Q/Q increase compared to the September-quarter. Despite the decline in balance sheet quality, the dividend is well-supported by net investment income and should be easily sustainable at a currently quarterly level of $0.48 per-share.

(Source: Author)

Ares Capital’s valuation

Ares Capital is valued at a premium to net asset value. Ares Capital is currently priced at a price-to-NAV ratio of 1.18X compared to a 3-year average P/B ratio of 1.06X. The surge in valuation is due mainly to the BDC delivering strong results on a consistent basis and being able to grow its portfolio through new loan originations. These new loans are going to generate interest income going forward, which means the BDC could even see an expansion in its excess dividend coverage.

The industry group includes BDCs with high (improving) balance sheet quality like Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) and FS KKR Capital (FSK). Blackstone Secured Lending has the best balance sheet quality in the industry group and therefore the highest P/NAV ratio... which is why I continue to recommend the BDC: A 10%-Yielding BDC Gem.

FS KKR Capital is fundamentally improving its portfolio mix and loan quality profile, which has resulted in a steady upwards revaluation as well: A 13% Yield And Repricing Upside.

In my last article on Ares Capital, I stated that:

I see a 1.10-1.15X price-to-NAV ratio as realistic and easily achievable for Ares Capital... which calculates to a fair value range of $21.75 to $22.75 per-share, implying up to 5% upside potential.

Ares Capital's net asset value grew 3% year-over-year to $19.89 as of the end of FY 2024. Assuming a 1.10-1.15X fair value P/NAV ratio, shares of Ares Capital may have a fair value between $21.88 and $22.87 per-share. They currently trade at $23.47 per share which makes them slightly overvalued.

Risks with Ares Capital

Ares Capital invests in loans that pay the BDC (and ultimately, its shareholders) variable rates. This means that a decline in federal fund rates, which is largely expected to happen in 2025, could potentially be a headwind to Ares Capital’s interest income. Since interest income represents the majority (71%) of total investment income for ARCC, falling interest rates could pose a valuation headwind.

Final thoughts

Ares Capital reported robust results for its fourth fiscal quarter. The BDC reported solid net investment income which easily supported Ares Capital's dividend and the investment firm saw some major growth in its portfolio value in the last year. This growth, in my opinion, has been reflected in the BDC's valuation. The dividend is well-supported by net investment income, despite an increase in the non-accrual percentage in the December quarter, and the 8% yield should be reasonably safe in FY 2025. However, shares are now a bit expensive, based off of Ares Capital's P/NAV ratio, and I would wait for a drop below $22.87 per-share before buying.

data.title: Wix.com Q4: Strong Margins, AI Efficiency, And A Bullish Road Ahead
data.description:
Wix (NASDAQ:WIX) has just posted its results. The numbers are interesting and a couple of underlying trends have caught my attention. Let's take a deep dive.

Wix's Business Model and Differentiation: Why It Works

For entrepreneurs to start creating a website with Wix is very simple. You can start for free, and if you ever need extra features or customization, upgrading is easy. While most of Wix's revenue comes from subscriptions, it's way more than just a website builder at this point. It has evolved into a completely online platform where users can not only create websites but actually run and grow their businesses without needing a dozen different tools. With Wix Payments, e-commerce solutions, and business tools all built-in, it helps users streamline everything in one place—no extra plugins or complicated integrations.

One of the reasons Wix works so well is how ridiculously easy it is to use. The drag-and-drop editor means anyone can put together a solid website, with no coding needed. If you've ever tried WordPress or Shopify, you know they can get technical fast. Wix skips the complexity and makes sure even beginners can get a great-looking site up and running quickly.

Then there's the AI factor. Instead of spending hours tweaking layouts and moving things around, Wix's AI-assisted tools do a lot of the heavy lifting. The AI Website Builder and smart sitemap generator help users design and optimize their sites almost instantly. This isn't just about making things faster—it's about removing all the frustrating parts of web design, so people can focus on what actually matters: their business.

What really makes Wix stand out, though, is that it doesn't box users into a single category. Some platforms are all about aesthetics, while others are built strictly for selling stuff. Wix? It does both. Web hosting, SEO, payments, marketing, automation—it's all in one place, so you don't have to jump between multiple services just to keep your site running.

At the end of the day, Wix is built for people who just want things to work without overcomplicating the process. Whether you're setting up a personal blog, launching an online store, or scaling a full-blown business, it gives you the tools to make it happen—without the usual headaches.

4Q24 Earnings

Wix posted interesting revenue growth in Q4 2024, fueled by a combination of higher bookings and strong demand for its premium subscriptions. Revenue jumped 14% year-over-year, reaching $460.5 million, this was largely due to price increases and a broader customer base.

The biggest improvement, however, came in operating margins. Non-GAAP operating margin expanded from 16% to 22%, a 6 percentage point jump, while profit margins soared from 0.7% to 10.4%. This is where Wix's scalability really shows. Because it's a software-as-a-service (SaaS) business, once the infrastructure is built, every additional customer costs relatively little to serve. The more users Wix acquires, the lower its cost per user, meaning that revenue grows faster than expenses.

Another key factor behind margin expansion was cost discipline. Even though Wix invested in AI and marketing, the operating cost base remained stable. The company is also benefiting from automation with customer service and website building increasingly being handled by AI.

Investment thesis

AI-Driven expansion fuels sustainable growth and margin expansion. Wix has successfully integrated AI across its platform. The AI Website Builder, AI visual sitemap generator, and automated design features lower the learning curve for users while allowing them to create a good product. On the cost side, AI is driving operational efficiencies, helping Wix scale profitably. Customer support automation, AI-powered marketing tools, and predictive analytics reduce overhead expenses, contributing to margin expansion. With operating margins already improving (+6pp YoY in Q4 2024), Wix is demonstrating SaaS-level scalability, where revenue outpaces cost growth. If these dynamics accelerate, Wix could see even stronger free cash flow generation.

Risks

While Wix is exhibiting very promising trends, there are several risks that could deny its ability to sustain momentum. One of the biggest concerns is AI execution risk. The company is betting heavily on AI-driven automation to improve efficiency and reduce costs, but there's no guarantee that users will fully adopt these features. If the AI-powered website builder and automation tools fail to gain traction the expected margin expansion could fall short. Additionally, while AI is very promising to leverage the company's platform, there is also a high associated cost. Competitors like Shopify, Squarespace, and Webflow are also integrating AI into their platforms, and if their implementations prove more effective, Wix will feel it negatively.

Another major risk lies in Wix's expansion into business solutions and e-commerce. While its Partners segment is growing rapidly, the agency and freelancer market is fragmented, and larger firms might still prefer WordPress, Webflow, or custom-built solutions. Meanwhile, its e-commerce ambitions put it in direct competition with Shopify, BigCommerce, and WooCommerce, all of which have stronger footholds in online retail.

Macroeconomic conditions present another layer of risk. Wix relies heavily on small businesses, which makes it vulnerable to economic downturns. In a recession, these users are likely to cut discretionary spending, which will likely include some of the company's products and could lead to a slowdown in new customer acquisition and higher churn.

Valuation

Wix's valuation seems, particularly when looking at its high forward P/E ratio (105.76x). However, this perception begins to shift when considering the company's expected EBITDA growth rate of 97.67% for the next year. This accelerating improvement opens the possibility that Wix is at an inflection point, transitioning from a company focused primarily on revenue growth to one that is beginning to get it translated into the bottom line.

That said, Wix's Price-to-Sales (P/S) ratio of 7.48x remains reasonable, as it is below high-growth names like Shopify. However, its P/E multiple starts to look misleading when considering the potential for EBITDA margins to expand. If this trend remains in place, Wix might "grow into" its valuation, meaning today's seemingly high multiple will decline as earnings grow.

Wrapping up

Wix is at a critical inflection point. The company is showing signs that it is transitioning from a pure growth-focused SaaS business to one that is now scaling margins and profitability. Its AI-driven automation is improving efficiency, allowing Wix to expand margins while growing revenue, a hallmark of high-quality SaaS companies.

Given the current estimates, we can build a Bull and Bear scenario. For that, we will use the December 2026 high and low estimates and we will consider a 56 multiple for the bull scenario (closer to Intuit) and 28 for the bear scenario (GoDaddy)

The results suggest a very positive risk/reward proposition, supporting a buy rating on the stock. Going forward, we should keep an eye on the evolution of the operating margins as a way to check that the business model in following the trend we outlined in our thesis.
